deliveryStandards: Treat this PRD as the source of truth for scope and completion. A story is not complete when it only adds typed helpers, mocks, or static examples if the story calls for a runnable app, service, installer, or workflow.
Do not mark a story passed unless the relevant surface can be run manually from this repository with documented commands.

Every story that changes behavior must add automated coverage. Minimum expectation: unit tests for new pure logic, integration tests for service and package boundaries, and end-to-end or smoke coverage for user-facing flows where practical.

Automated tests for product behavior must live with the product code or in a clearly named top-level product test suite. Ralph-only tests are useful but are not sufficient as the sole proof of completion.
When a story changes setup, architecture, commands, or user workflows, the README and any relevant supporting docs must be updated in the same story.

This phase targets a single-user self-hosted system. Auth can remain token-based, but tokens and bootstrap secrets must be configurable through environment variables or config files and must not be hardcoded as the only production path.

This phase may use JSON persistence if it is durable enough for restart recovery, well documented, and covered by tests. If JSON persistence becomes a limiter, SQLite is the preferred next persistence step.
A story that introduces a client application must deliver an actual runnable client package with explicit local run commands, not only a client SDK.
A story that introduces operational behavior must include failure handling, useful errors, and at least one documented manual smoke test path.

Client surfaces must not fail as silent blank windows or blank pages. If the renderer cannot boot, the user should see a visible failure state and the error should be logged in the environment-appropriate way.

Desktop builds and packaged desktop artifacts must be compatible with the actual load mode they use, including file-based loads for Electron or packaged shells. Asset URLs, preload wiring, and startup assumptions must be tested in that mode instead of only through a browser dev server.

If a story claims an end-to-end user-facing workflow, the acceptance proof must exercise the real workflow from the user surface through the backing service behavior. API-only plumbing or mocked records are not enough unless the story is explicitly API-only.

Browser-based clients must work when served from a different development origin than the control plane. Authenticated API requests and live event delivery must support required browser cross-origin behavior, including CORS preflight for Authorization-bearing requests.
For this single-user phase, onboarding should prefer securely generated credentials and pairing flows over committed defaults or repeated manual token copy/paste wherever practical.

Web, desktop, and mobile clients should share one coherent operator mental model: navigate sessions, inspect a selected session, issue prompts or follow-up instructions, review outputs, and take actions without dropping to raw API calls for normal use.

The primary operator UI should be a focused workspace rather than a dashboard of unrelated cards. Favor a persistent navigation rail, a dominant selected-session transcript pane, an anchored composer, and collapsible secondary inspectors or settings panels for less-frequent controls.

Host onboarding, provider readiness, pairing, advanced diagnostics, packaging details, and other setup-heavy controls may live behind settings panes, drawers, sheets, or dedicated setup views instead of occupying the main session workspace at all times.

A runnable development client is not the same as a distributable client. If the product is expected to be installed outside the repo, packaging and install artifacts must be called out explicitly in the relevant story.

Provider integrations used by runtime-backed sessions must support the intended non-interactive execution model for this product. A provider is not considered implemented if it only works in an interactive TTY that the product does not supply.

Core operator workflows should be possible from the client surfaces themselves. If setup or supervision still requires raw curl commands or hidden ids for normal use, that gap should be called out explicitly in the relevant story.

Only the active story may change its own passes or notes fields. If a later story changes code paths or user flows covered by earlier completed stories, the current story must document which earlier stories were impacted and what regression checks were rerun.
Stories that touch shared server, runtime, auth, or client infrastructure must extend regression coverage for previously completed behavior they affect.
Do not consider the PRD run complete unless the final branch tip passes the full verification command with all stories integrated together.
If the final integrated branch fails verification, leave the remaining work explicitly unfinished in the PRD instead of claiming full completion.
PRD notes for completed stories should name the exact automated tests and verification commands that were rerun, especially when the story changed shared behavior or revalidated an earlier story.

Prefer vertically sliced stories for each operator surface. Do not split essential parts of the same web, mobile, or desktop workflow into separate follow-up stories when the user would experience them as one feature.
Final hardening or release-readiness stories must have strictly lower priority than every unfinished implementation story they depend on.
When a story relies on prior backend or shared-package capabilities, record that relationship in a dependsOn field and keep the prerequisite at an earlier priority tier.

userStories.title: Create a pnpm TypeScript monorepo
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want the project organized as a pnpm monorepo with TypeScript-first tooling so that server, runtime, web, mobile, desktop, and shared packages can evolve together.
acceptanceCriteria: The repository uses pnpm workspaces for all first-party apps and packages
All first-party apps and packages build with TypeScript
Shared linting, formatting, and tsconfig rules are centralized
Root commands exist for install, build, lint, typecheck, and test across the full monorepo
The root verification flow is documented and intended to be run locally and in CI

userStories.title: Create shared core packages
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want shared packages for protocol, auth, sessions, ports, providers, and UI primitives so that all clients and services use the same domain model.
acceptanceCriteria: The monorepo includes shared packages for protocol, auth, sessions, ports, providers, and UI
Server, runtime, and clients consume shared types instead of duplicating models
Package boundaries are documented
Changes in shared types are validated by typecheck across all dependent apps
Shared packages expose runtime-safe exports that match their declared package entrypoints

userStories.title: Run a Remote Dev Console Server control plane
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a central server that manages authentication, hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, notifications, and port exposure so that all clients connect through one control plane.
acceptanceCriteria: The server exposes APIs for hosts, workspaces, sessions, approvals, notifications, and forwarded ports
The server supports real-time event delivery to connected clients
The server persists core metadata required to recover state after restart
Unauthorized access to protected resources is rejected
Single-user operator tokens and bootstrap tokens are configurable through environment variables or config files and are not hardcoded as the only usable path
The server supports a secure first-run or bootstrap path that can generate high-entropy operator and bootstrap tokens automatically when explicit tokens are not provided

Generated credentials are persisted in a documented local config or state location intended for the operator, with documented file-permission or workstation-storage expectations and a non-copy-pasted retrieval path
The server has an explicit local run command and documented configuration
The control plane has integration tests that exercise auth, persistence, and real-time event delivery
Authenticated browser clients served from a separate origin can connect to the control plane, including CORS preflight handling for Authorization-bearing API requests and live event delivery

userStories.title: Install the runtime on a remote Linux host
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to install a runtime on a remote Linux machine so that coding agents can run next to the repository and services they need.
acceptanceCriteria: A documented install flow is available for supported Linux systems
The runtime can enroll with the server using a bootstrap token
The runtime reports version, health, and connectivity status
The install flow is safe to rerun on an already configured host
The repo includes a concrete installer or install helper that matches the documented flow
The install and enrollment flow has automated integration coverage

userStories.title: Establish secure single-user credential bootstrap and pairing
userStories.description: As a single-user operator, I want the control plane to generate and manage my credentials safely and let me pair a mobile client quickly so that I do not rely on hardcoded tokens or repeated manual token entry.
acceptanceCriteria: A first-run or explicit bootstrap flow can generate high-entropy operator and bootstrap tokens automatically when they are not already configured.
Generated credentials are stored in a documented local file or workstation-backed secure location with appropriate single-user protection expectations.
The operator has a documented way to inspect, rotate, or reissue credentials without editing source files.
At least one operator surface can produce a mobile pairing payload, such as a QR code or equivalent signed bootstrap blob, that lets the mobile app connect without retyping the token.
The server and shared auth or session layers expose the contracts needed for web, mobile, and desktop clients to restore saved operator connections across restarts.
Automated coverage proves the bootstrap flow, reissue path, and pairing payload generation end to end, and docs cover setup, storage location, and recovery path.
implementationNotes: This story establishes the secure single-user auth foundation. Client-specific storage and UI must still be completed inside the web, mobile, and desktop client stories.

userStories.title: Register repositories as managed workspaces
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to register local or remote repository paths as workspaces so that sessions always execute against a known codebase.
acceptanceCriteria: A workspace can be created from a repository path on a registered host
Workspace metadata includes host, path, default branch, and runtime association
Invalid or inaccessible paths are rejected with a clear error
Users can list, inspect, and remove registered workspaces
Workspace registration validates that the target path is an accessible git repository
Client surfaces can create a workspace without requiring the operator to drop to raw API calls for normal setup
implementationNotes: A workspace is the registered working directory for sessions, not just an opaque ID. Client surfaces should show the repo path clearly so operators understand what directory the session will run in.
The intended operator flow is: choose a host, enter or select a repository path, register the workspace, then start or monitor sessions against that workspace.

userStories.title: Start and monitor coding agent sessions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to start sessions against a workspace and monitor them from web, desktop, or mobile so that I do not need to SSH into the machine to supervise work.
acceptanceCriteria: A session can be started against a chosen workspace and provider
A session includes the operator's initial task or prompt, and clients can send follow-up prompts or instructions to an existing session
Clients receive live status, logs, and output events
Sessions can be paused, canceled, and resumed
Disconnected clients can reconnect and recover active session state
The core session lifecycle has integration coverage across the server and runtime boundary
implementationNotes: Do not satisfy this story with session records that only contain ids and provider selection. The runtime must receive the actual task input needed to perform work.
The session detail model should preserve the original prompt and subsequent operator instructions so client transcripts can show what the agent was asked to do.

userStories.title: Support Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode providers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a common provider adapter interface so that Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode can all run through the same session model.
acceptanceCriteria: The runtime exposes a provider adapter interface
Claude Code can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
Codex can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
OpenCode can be launched and monitored through the adapter interface
Provider-specific failures are surfaced without crashing the session manager
Each provider adapter has contract coverage for launch, output capture, exit handling, and failure reporting
Runtime-backed provider launches work in the actual non-interactive execution environment used by the product, not only in an interactive TTY or fixture process
implementationNotes: If a provider requires a special non-interactive subcommand, prompt format, environment variable, or approval mode, the adapter must encode that explicitly instead of assuming the interactive CLI default will work.
At least one automated or documented proof should use the real provider CLI path for each supported provider where feasible, especially for Codex, so headless launch regressions are caught.

userStories.title: Create isolated worktrees per task
userStories.description: As a developer, I want sessions to optionally run in dedicated git worktrees so that multiple agents can work in parallel without stepping on each other.
acceptanceCriteria: A session can create a dedicated worktree before starting
Worktree metadata is linked to the session
Dirty worktrees are rejected by default unless explicitly allowed
Users can choose direct workspace mode for simple workflows
The implementation includes cleanup behavior or documented cleanup commands for stale worktrees

userStories.title: Review diffs, changed files, and patch summaries
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to review agent output from the client so that I can validate changes without leaving Remote Dev Console.
acceptanceCriteria: Clients can list changed files for a session
Clients can view diffs or patch summaries
Large diffs degrade gracefully through truncation or pagination
The change view clearly identifies added, modified, renamed, and removed files
At least one user-facing client exercises the diff-review flow in an integration or smoke test

userStories.title: Approve or reject privileged actions
userStories.description: As a developer, I want risky actions to require explicit approval so that agents cannot perform sensitive operations without review.
acceptanceCriteria: Providers can raise approval-required actions through a common interface
Clients can approve or reject pending actions
Approval decisions are stored in an audit log
Rejected actions are surfaced cleanly to the running session
The approval flow is covered by integration tests from request through decision and session outcome

userStories.title: Forward remote ports for previews and tools
userStories.description: As a developer, I want built-in remote port forwarding so that I can access preview apps, dashboards, and dev services from any client device.
acceptanceCriteria: Users can manually forward a TCP port from a registered host
Clients can view all active forwarded ports for a workspace or session
Forwarded ports can be opened, closed, and expired
HTTP services are exposed through a managed URL
At least private and shared visibility modes are supported
The port-forwarding lifecycle has integration coverage including expiration behavior

userStories.title: Persist audit records and expose audit APIs
userStories.description: As a maintainer or administrator, I want an audit trail for session and access activity so that sensitive remote actions can be reviewed later.
acceptanceCriteria: The audit log records session lifecycle events, approvals, provider choice, execution mode selection, workspace path, and port exposure changes.
Each entry includes actor, target, timestamp, and outcome.
Authorized clients can inspect audit records through an explicit API with filtering or pagination suitable for client inspectors.
Critical security-relevant events are not silently dropped.
The audit log survives restart and is covered by integration tests.
This story is responsible for persistence and API shape; client inspection UX belongs inside the web, mobile, and desktop client stories.
implementationNotes: Do not claim this story complete if audit data exists only as internal server state.
Client-side audit panes should be implemented with the client stories rather than deferred back into this backend story.

userStories.title: Support local mode with the same runtime contract
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the same runtime model to work locally so that local and remote development follow the same session and workspace lifecycle.
acceptanceCriteria: A local runtime can register with the server or attach directly in development mode
Local workspaces support the same session APIs as remote workspaces
Providers do not require separate local-only implementations
Clients clearly distinguish local and remote hosts
Local and remote flows share the same core runtime abstractions and tests prove the parity
At least one user-facing client can create or monitor a local-mode session through the same control-plane or runtime contract used for remote sessions
implementationNotes: Do not satisfy this story by adding a separate mock-only local client path. The same runtime session lifecycle should back both local and remote flows.
The acceptance proof should show a real local repository path, a real local runtime mode, and the resulting local session appearing distinctly in a client surface.

userStories.title: Auto-detect common development ports
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the runtime to detect common listening ports so that previews are easy to find and expose when needed.
acceptanceCriteria: The runtime can detect listening ports associated with active sessions or workspaces
Detected ports appear in clients with suggested labels when possible
Users can promote a detected port into a managed forward
Detected ports are not exposed externally until a user forwards them
Detection and promotion flows are covered by automated integration tests
Automated coverage uses an actual listening process or server so detection is proven end to end rather than by seeded detected-port records
At least one client surface can display a detected port and promote it into a managed forward in an integrated test or smoke flow
implementationNotes: This story is not complete if the server can list synthetic detected ports but promotion fails from that exact list result.
The key proof is: start a real listening service, detect it, show it in a client or API response, promote that exact detected port, and successfully reach the managed URL afterward.

userStories.title: Persist session history and resume across devices
userStories.description: As a developer, I want sessions to be resumable from web, desktop, or mobile so that I can move between devices without losing context.
acceptanceCriteria: Session metadata includes provider, workspace, timestamps, and current state
Clients can reopen an existing session from another device
Recovered sessions include recent logs and messages
Temporary disconnects do not destroy active session state
Recovery behavior is covered by automated integration tests across more than one client surface

userStories.title: Deliver the web operator client
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a browser-based client so that I can manage sessions, approvals, ports, and previews from any machine.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable web app package with explicit local run and build commands.
Users can sign in from a browser, restore that saved operator connection across browser restarts for the single-user scope, and do not need to re-enter the token during normal use.

The web client can create and register a workspace by choosing a host and repository path, clearly shows the resulting working directory for sessions, and includes the normal workspace selection flow without requiring raw IDs.

The web client includes host onboarding and runtime readiness surfaces so an operator can add or pair a host, inspect connectivity or install state, and understand provider readiness without leaving the client.

The web client presents a focused operator workspace with a persistent navigation rail for repositories or sessions, a dominant selected-session transcript pane, and an anchored composer for starting sessions and sending follow-up instructions.

The selected session view shows the original prompt, follow-up prompts, streamed output, approval state, diff review, preview links, execution mode details, workspace path, provider choice, and actionable failure diagnostics.

The web client keeps secondary detail surfaces such as audit inspection, approval queues, port inspectors, runtime diagnostics, and setup flows in collapsible side panels, drawers, sheets, or dedicated views rather than forcing them all into the primary workspace at once.
The web client includes an audit-oriented inspector for the current session and related approvals, ports, or host actions rather than leaving audit inspection as API-only.
The web client supports opt-in browser notifications for approval-required, failed-session, and completed-session events, including category controls and deep links back into the relevant session.
The web client can review diffs, respond to approvals, open forwarded HTTP previews, and display detected ports with promotion into managed forwards.

The web client has browser-focused integration or end-to-end coverage for the full operator flow, including workspace creation, onboarding/readiness, prompt composition, approvals, diagnostics, notifications, and audit inspection.
The web client has a visible top-level error state for renderer startup failures so browser users do not see an unexplained blank page.
implementationNotes: Treat this as the complete browser operator product slice. Do not defer core workflow pieces such as notifications, diagnostics, or audit visibility into separate browser-only follow-up stories.
The browser client must work from a separate development origin against the control plane with authenticated API requests and event streaming.
The visual direction should feel like a native operator tool: restrained chrome, dark high-density workspace, strong hierarchy around the transcript, and minimal dashboard clutter.

userStories.title: Deliver the mobile operator client with Expo
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a mobile app built with React Native and Expo so that I can monitor work, approve actions, and open previews from my phone.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable Expo app with documented local run commands.

The mobile app can pair with the single-user control plane using the generated pairing flow, persist that connection securely enough for the self-hosted scope, and restore it across normal app restarts.
The mobile app can create a workspace by selecting a host and entering or importing a repository path, and it clearly shows what working directory a session will use.

The mobile app exposes host onboarding and runtime readiness information in a phone-appropriate flow so an operator can pair a host, inspect connectivity or install state, and understand provider readiness without raw API calls.

The mobile app adapts the same core operator model as web and desktop to a phone-friendly layout: session list or inbox navigation, selected-session transcript, touch-friendly prompt composer, quick approvals, preview access, and session controls.

The selected session view shows the original prompt, follow-up prompts, streamed output, approval state, execution mode, provider, workspace path, and actionable failure diagnostics in a mobile-appropriate presentation.
The mobile app exposes an audit-oriented inspector for the current session and related approvals, ports, or host actions rather than leaving those details to raw APIs.

The mobile app keeps secondary or infrequent controls such as host setup, pairing details, provider readiness, and advanced diagnostics in drawers, tabs, sheets, or settings flows so the main session workspace stays focused.
The mobile app supports opt-in notifications for approval-required, failed-session, and completed-session events, and notification taps can reopen the related session where the platform allows it.
The mobile app can approve or reject pending actions, open forwarded previews, and surface detected ports or other related session artifacts that matter for operator decisions.

The mobile app has smoke or integration coverage for the primary operator flow, including pairing, workspace creation, prompt composition, approvals, diagnostics, notifications, and recovery after reconnect.
The mobile app has a visible top-level error state for renderer startup failures so mobile users do not land on an unexplained blank screen.
implementationNotes: Mobile should preserve the same operator mental model as web and desktop, but it may adapt layout and controls to stacked panes, tabs, drawers, or bottom sheets.
Do not treat QR or pairing import as a standalone feature disconnected from the normal mobile operator experience.
Keep the session transcript and composer as the visual center of the mobile experience, with secondary setup and inspection surfaces tucked behind explicit navigation.

userStories.title: Deliver the desktop operator client
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a desktop app so that I can use Remote Dev Console as a primary control surface for both remote and local work.
acceptanceCriteria: The repo includes an actual runnable desktop app package with a real launchable desktop window, not only a desktop client library.
The desktop app can connect to the server, manage sessions, and operate both local and remote runtime flows from one interface.

The desktop app can create and register a workspace by choosing a host and repository path, clearly shows the workspace path as the working directory for sessions, and lets users switch between local and remote workspaces without raw IDs.

The desktop app includes host onboarding and runtime readiness surfaces so an operator can pair a host, inspect connectivity or install state, and understand provider readiness directly from the desktop client.

The desktop app presents a focused desktop operator workspace with a narrow persistent navigation rail for repositories or sessions, a dominant selected-session transcript pane, a bottom-anchored composer, and a slim top toolbar for branch, session, or workspace context.

The selected session view shows the original prompt, follow-up prompts, streamed output, audit information, execution mode, provider, workspace path, and actionable failure diagnostics that help an operator recover.
The desktop app stores its token or connection secrets in a desktop-appropriate secure location for a single-user setup and restores the saved connection across app restarts.

The desktop app keeps secondary surfaces such as approvals, audit inspection, port details, runtime diagnostics, onboarding, readiness, and advanced settings in collapsible sidebars, drawers, inspectors, or settings panes instead of making the main window a dashboard of equal-weight cards.
The desktop app includes an audit-oriented inspector for the current session and related approvals, ports, or host actions rather than leaving those details to raw APIs.
The desktop app supports operator notifications for approval-required, failed-session, and completed-session events in a desktop-appropriate way, with deep links into the session where supported.

The desktop app has smoke or integration coverage for the primary operator flow, including secure reconnect, workspace creation, onboarding/readiness, prompt composition, approvals, diagnostics, notifications, and preview access.

Desktop dev and packaged builds must prove that the renderer loads real visible content when opened through the actual Electron or packaged file-based entrypoint, not only through browser-hosted dev assets.
The desktop app has a visible top-level error state for renderer startup failures so operators do not see a blank window without explanation.
implementationNotes: Treat this as the full desktop product slice rather than a shell plus later follow-up UX stories.

The desktop client should feel like a native operator workstation: dark, compact, transcript-first, with the primary visual emphasis on the active session and composer instead of on a grid of control panels.

Use hierarchy intentionally: left rail for navigation, center pane for conversation and work, right-side contextual inspection when needed, and a separate settings surface for setup-heavy or rarely used controls.

userStories.title: Enforce integrated product verification and regression coverage
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want product-owned unit, integration, and end-to-end tests so that story completion is validated by the application itself rather than only by Ralph.
acceptanceCriteria: Core shared packages have unit tests for their business logic.
Server and runtime flows have integration tests that exercise real package boundaries.
Primary operator journeys for web, mobile, and desktop have smoke or end-to-end coverage where practical.
The root verification command runs product-owned tests in addition to Ralph loop tests and fails on product regressions.
Test ownership and test scope are documented so future stories extend the right test layer.
Stories that modify shared behavior update the relevant regression tests instead of relying only on new story-specific tests.

The verification suite is stable enough that known timing-sensitive tests are either hardened or explicitly isolated and documented; a story should not be considered complete when the main verification gate is routinely flaky.
Client verification includes startup coverage that catches silent blank-page or blank-window failures, including Electron or packaged file-entry loads where applicable.
implementationNotes: This story is about the product verification system, not just adding more tests. The final result should make later client and hardening stories trustworthy.
For desktop, prefer checks that assert the built HTML references compatible relative assets for file-based loads and at least one smoke path that proves the window renders visible UI content.

userStories.title: Document operator, developer, and packaging workflows
userStories.description: As a developer, I want clear documentation so that I can self-host the project, connect runtimes, run the clients, and contribute to the pnpm monorepo.
acceptanceCriteria: The root README includes a quickstart for install, local run commands, verification commands, the main repo layout, and the current Ralph attempt workflow.
Documentation covers server deployment, runtime install, workspace creation, client setup, and operator onboarding for web, mobile, and desktop.
Documentation explains provider setup for Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode, including the non-interactive runtime expectations of the product.
Documentation explains port forwarding, detected port promotion, auth expectations, security boundaries, audit visibility, and recovery behavior.
Documentation explains single-user credential bootstrap, storage, retrieval, rotation or reissue, and mobile pairing.

Documentation describes the shared client layout and information architecture for web, desktop, and the mobile-adapted operator workspace, including which controls belong in the main workspace versus drawers, inspectors, or settings.
Documentation explains packaging commands, output locations, environment requirements, and any remaining signing or store-submission prerequisites.
README and supporting docs honestly distinguish what is production-ready, what is MVP, and what is still incomplete.
README smoke commands and run commands reflect the branch tip exactly and are validated by automated tests or smoke checks.
Documentation includes a troubleshooting section for client startup failures such as blank pages, blank windows, asset-path mismatches, and renderer boot errors.
implementationNotes: This story replaces the old split between general docs and README polish. Keep one coherent docs story so operators and contributors do not get conflicting guidance.

userStories.title: Produce distributable desktop and mobile app packages
userStories.description: As an operator, I want installable desktop and mobile artifacts so that I can use the clients without cloning the repo and running development commands.
acceptanceCriteria: The desktop client can produce a distributable artifact such as a macOS app bundle, DMG, ZIP, or equivalent installer-ready package from the repo

The mobile client has a documented and runnable packaging path for device-installable artifacts or store-ready builds, such as an APK, IPA, or EAS build profile, rather than only Expo development mode
Packaging commands, output locations, and environment requirements are documented for each supported client artifact
The packaged clients preserve the intended operator experience, including sign-in, workspace creation, session management, and preview access
Automated coverage or documented smoke flows prove that packaged builds launch successfully and can connect to the control plane in at least one supported desktop path and one supported mobile path
Desktop packaging proof includes checks against the real packaged or file-based entrypoint so asset-path or renderer-boot regressions do not ship as blank windows
Any remaining signing, notarization, store-submission, or vendor account prerequisites are called out explicitly instead of being implied as complete
implementationNotes: This story is about installable artifacts, not just successful TypeScript builds.
A practical MVP can target one desktop platform first, but the output should be something an operator can install or unpack without running the repo in dev mode.
For mobile, it is acceptable to document a concrete EAS or equivalent build pipeline if the resulting artifact path and operator steps are real and tested.

userStories.title: Run a final regression and release-readiness pass
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want a final integration hardening pass so that the branch only claims completion when the combined system still works end to end after all stories land.
acceptanceCriteria: The full root verification command passes on the final branch tip after all completed stories are integrated together.

Key operator flows are revalidated end to end: server startup, credential bootstrap, workspace registration, session lifecycle, approvals, diff review, forwarded ports, detected port promotion, notifications, audit inspection, runtime onboarding, packaged-client launch smoke paths, session recovery, and browser-client sign-in from a separate local development origin.
Any regressions introduced by later stories are fixed before the PRD is marked complete.
The final PRD notes identify which high-risk earlier stories were revalidated during the hardening pass.
README and supporting docs reflect the final integrated behavior rather than only per-story local changes.
This story runs only after all other intended implementation stories for the branch are complete, including packaging.
Final hardening explicitly revalidates that web and desktop clients render visible content instead of blank pages or blank windows in their real launch modes.
implementationNotes: This is a hardening and final-validation story, not a substitute for unfinished feature stories.
It may make small integration fixes, but it should not be used to declare missing major capabilities acceptable.
Browser sign-in proof should include a real cross-origin request path, not just same-origin helpers that bypass browser preflight behavior.
Single-user auth proof should cover generated credential storage or retrieval plus mobile pairing import instead of relying only on manually typed tokens.
